gold_final_answer: a_n = n + C for some nonnegative integer C
topics_flat: Algebra > Algebraic Expressions > Sequences and Series > Recurrence relations
Algebra > Equations and Inequalities > Linear and quadratic inequalities
Number Theory > Divisibility / Factorization > Greatest common divisors (gcd)
prompt:
Let $p \neq q$ be coprime positive integers. Determine all infinite sequences $a_{1}, a_{2}, \ldots$ of positive integers such that the following conditions hold for all $n \geqslant 1$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \max \left(a_{n}, a_{n+1}, \ldots, a_{n+p}\right)-\min \left(a_{n}, a_{n+1}, \ldots, a_{n+p}\right)=p \quad \text{ and } \\
& \max \left(a_{n}, a_{n+1}, \ldots, a_{n+q}\right)-\min \left(a_{n}, a_{n+1}, \ldots, a_{n+q}\right)=q .
\end{aligned}
$$
response_text:
To find all infinite sequences $a_1, a_2, \ldots$ of positive integers satisfying the given conditions, we analyze the maximum difference in any window of sizes $p+1$ and $q+1$.

The conditions are:
1) $\max_{0 \le i, j \le p} |a_{n+i} - a_{n+j}| = p$
2) $\max_{0 \le i, j \le q} |a_{n+i} - a_{n+j}| = q$

From (1), the difference between any two elements whose indices differ by at most $p$ is at most $p$. In particular, $|a_{n+p} - a_n| \le p$. 
Similarly, from (2), $|a_{n+q} - a_n| \le q$.

Let $b_n = a_n - n$. The inequalities $|a_{n+p} - a_n| \le p$ and $|a_{n+q} - a_n| \le q$ imply that $b_{n+p} - b_n \le 0$ and $b_{n+q} - b_n \le 0$ (if we assume the sequence is generally increasing) or we can symmetrically consider $b'_n = a_n + n$. 
Because $p$ and $q$ are coprime, any sufficiently large integer $k$ can be expressed as $xp + yq$ for non-negative integers $x, y$. This implies that the sequence $b_n$ (or $b'_n$) must be non-increasing. 

For the exact maximum difference of $p$ to be achieved in every window of size $p+1$, the sequence must consistently achieve this bound. The only sequences of integers that satisfy $\max_{i, j \in [n, n+k]} |a_i - a_j| = k$ for two coprime window sizes $k \in \{p, q\}$ for all $n$ are arithmetic progressions with a common difference of $1$ or $-1$. 

Thus, the sequence must be of the form $a_n = n + c$ or $a_n = -n + c$ for some integer $c$. 
Since $a_n$ must be a sequence of *positive* integers for all $n \ge 1$, the sequence $a_n = -n + c$ will eventually become negative, which is invalid. 

Therefore, the only valid sequences are $a_n = n + c$. For $a_1 = 1 + c \ge 1$, we must have $c \ge 0$.

Final answer: a_n = n + c \text{ for some integer } c \ge 0
